summary: Gruber responds to critics who cited the GIF patent debacle as a reason Mozilla should refuse to support H.264 video. He argues the GIF and H.264 situations are fundamentally different: GIF was unknowingly built on a patented algorithm owned by a single company, while H.264 is managed by an industry consortium (MPEG LA) with clear licensing terms. He contends that Ogg Theora actually more closely resembles GIF's patent vulnerability, since it lacks the protection of a patent pool and could face submarine patent claims. He concludes that practical concerns, not idealism, drive format adoption, and that Mozilla's position is inconsistent as long as Firefox still supports Flash.
key_points: The GIF-H.264 analogy is flawed: Unisys was a single entity asserting a patent after the fact, while MPEG LA is an industry consortium with transparent licensing and incentive to behave predictably.
Ogg Theora is actually more vulnerable to patent claims than H.264, since it lacks patent pool protection and MPEG LA has suggested its patents may cover Theora too.
If MPEG LA raises licensing fees in 2016, web publishers will simply switch formats, just as they moved from GIF to PNG.
Mozilla's idealist stance is undermined by continuing to support Flash, which serves H.264 video through a proprietary plugin.
Practical concerns, not idealism, historically drive web publishers to adopt new formats.
money_quotes: Other than adding to the pile of evidence that software patents suck and are a drain on the industry, there isn't that much similarity between the GIF and H.264 situations.
It seems to me it's in their interests to behave consistently and predictably, not capriciously and opportunistically.
I'm just saying Ogg is a lot closer to being in GIF's boat than H.264 is.
I don't like the idea that the standard format for web video isn't a free, open standard like PNG or JPEG are for graphics. But that's the way it is.
Firefox users will continue to be served video using Flash — often encoded as H.264.
I think history shows that practical concerns, not idealism, is what drives web publishers to adopt new formats.
They can't say Firefox only supports free and open video formats while still supporting Flash.
key_insight: The GIF patent analogy actually argues against Ogg Theora more than H.264, because Theora lacks patent pool protection while H.264's consortium structure provides clear licensing and legal cover for users.
